core_thesis: @fakeplasticbrands argues that creativity is a disciplined, learnable practice fueled by deliberate research, cultural exposure, and cross-disciplinary thinking — not innate talent. The creator packages applied creative frameworks (ideation methods, pitch skills, taste cultivation) alongside curated cultural references (design archives, film, print). The through-line is that sharpening perception and building a personal visual/intellectual archive is what separates interesting work from generic output.
key_ideas: Creativity is a learnable, cultivated practice — not genetic or fixed
Research and accumulated knowledge are the hidden engine behind intuitive ideation
Cross-disciplinary problem-solving: applying an unrelated skill set to a field produces genuinely novel solutions
Pitching and persuasion are as important as creative output itself — selling ideas is a core creative skill
Archive diving and deliberate cultural exposure (museums, film, print) as taste infrastructure
Imperfection and analog texture (grain, inkbleed, dithering) as intentional design tools rather than flaws to fix
Hyper-extraction and commodification: the same human instinct that built civilization now strips and monetizes individual identity
Novelty comes from perspective shifts, not originality from scratch — the unusual framing is always nearby
